questions.question: When fine-tuning EfficientNet for a medical imaging task with only 800 training images, which layers should be frozen and which should be trained?
options: All layers should be unfrozen and trained from scratch for medical domain accuracy.

Freeze all layers except the final classification head initially. The early-to-middle layers of ImageNet-pretrained models learn universal visual features (edges, textures, shapes) that transfer well even to medical images (X-rays, histology). With only 800 images, training too many parameters causes severe overfitting. After convergence of just the classifier, optionally unfreeze the last 1-2 blocks for incremental improvement.
Freeze only the first layer; all other layers should be trainable.
Unfreeze the entire network but use a very small learning rate of 1e-8 to prevent catastrophic forgetting.
questions.answer: Freeze all layers except the final classification head initially. The early-to-middle layers of ImageNet-pretrained models learn universal visual features (edges, textures, shapes) that transfer well even to medical images (X-rays, histology). With only 800 images, training too many parameters causes severe overfitting. After convergence of just the classifier, optionally unfreeze the last 1-2 blocks for incremental improvement.
questions.explanation: Rule of thumb: fewer training images → freeze more layers. 800 images: freeze all but classifier (64K params). 8,000 images: unfreeze last block (500K params). 80,000 images: fine-tune all layers with small learning rate. The 'catastrophic forgetting' risk increases with more unfrozen layers.

questions.question: Why is data augmentation (random flips, crops, color jitter) particularly critical for small vision datasets, and why should you NOT apply augmentation to validation data?
options: Augmentation is critical because it doubles the dataset size, which improves accuracy linearly.

Data augmentation artificially increases dataset diversity by showing the model the same image in many realistic variations — teaching invariance to position, orientation, and color. This reduces overfitting when the training set is small. Validation data must NOT be augmented (only normalized) because validation measures generalization to real-world images as they actually appear — augmenting would make the task artificially harder and produce misleading metrics.
Data augmentation reduces training time by allowing smaller batch sizes.
Augmentation ensures all images have the same pixel intensity distribution.
questions.answer: Data augmentation artificially increases dataset diversity by showing the model the same image in many realistic variations — teaching invariance to position, orientation, and color. This reduces overfitting when the training set is small. Validation data must NOT be augmented (only normalized) because validation measures generalization to real-world images as they actually appear — augmenting would make the task artificially harder and produce misleading metrics.
questions.explanation: A model trained on 1000 cat images + 6 augmentation operations per image effectively sees 7000 diverse examples. Crucially, augmentation during training doesn't increase validation accuracy artificially — it reduces overfitting, so train/val accuracy gap narrows.

questions.question: What is the primary reason Vision Transformers (ViT) outperform CNNs on large datasets but underperform on small ones?
options: ViT requires more GPU memory, which causes out-of-memory issues on small datasets.

CNNs have inductive biases baked into their architecture (translation equivariance, local receptive fields) that are useful priors for vision tasks — allowing them to learn efficiently from limited data. ViT treats image patches as sequences with no inductive bias toward spatial locality; it must learn these spatial relationships purely from data. With enough data (millions of images), ViT learns better representations. With little data, CNNs' spatial inductive biases provide a crucial head start.
ViT uses more augmentation by default, which hurts performance on small datasets.
ViT's patch tokenization loses pixel-level detail that CNNs preserve with convolutional filters.
questions.answer: CNNs have inductive biases baked into their architecture (translation equivariance, local receptive fields) that are useful priors for vision tasks — allowing them to learn efficiently from limited data. ViT treats image patches as sequences with no inductive bias toward spatial locality; it must learn these spatial relationships purely from data. With enough data (millions of images), ViT learns better representations. With little data, CNNs' spatial inductive biases provide a crucial head start.
questions.explanation: The original ViT paper found ViT underperformed ResNet on ImageNet (1.2M images) but dramatically outperformed it when pre-trained on JFT-300M (300M images). The crossover point is roughly 10-100M images — below this, CNN inductive biases win.
